content.text: User correction is how an agent learns what the user actually wanted when the first response missed the mark. There are five correction patterns: editing a past user message and re-running the conversation from that point, undoing the last turn to try again, branching the conversation at a decision point to explore a different direction, regenerating the last assistant response with a fresh sample, and inline feedback chips that apply a quick correction prompt. Each pattern has a different scope: edit and undo change history, branch forks history, regenerate retries in place, and feedback chips append a correction without changing history.

attrs.alt: Five user correction patterns: edit and retry, branch conversation, undo last turn, regenerate, and inline feedback chips

content.text: Correction operations require mutating conversation history. Model history as a list of typed turns rather than a flat list of dicts so you can edit, truncate, and branch by turn ID without ambiguity.
Server API: Edit, Undo, Branch, Regenerate

Expose four endpoints that mutate the stored conversation and, where needed, call Claude to produce a new response. All history mutations happen on the server so they are authoritative and the client state can be a simple reflection of what the server returns.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

app = FastAPI()
client = AsyncAnthropic()
sessions: dict[str, Conversation] = {}

@app.post("/chat/{session_id}/edit-turn")
async def edit_turn(session_id: str, body: dict):
    """Edit a user turn and get a fresh assistant response."""
    conv = sessions.get(session_id)
    if not conv:
        return {"error": "Session not found"}, 404

    turn_id     = body["turn_id"]
    new_content = body["new_content"]

    # Truncate history at the edited turn
    conv = conv.edit_turn(turn_id, new_content)
    sessions[session_id] = conv

    # Re-run Claude from the new history
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=2048,
        messages=conv.to_messages(),
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text

    import uuid
    conv.turns.append(ConversationTurn(
        role="assistant", content=reply, turn_id=str(uuid.uuid4())
    ))
    sessions[session_id] = conv
    return {"reply": reply, "history": conv.to_messages()}

@app.post("/chat/{session_id}/undo")
async def undo_last(session_id: str):
    """Remove the last user+assistant pair."""
    conv = sessions.get(session_id)
    if not conv:
        return {"error": "Session not found"}, 404
    conv = conv.undo_last_turn()
    sessions[session_id] = conv
    return {"history": conv.to_messages()}

@app.post("/chat/{session_id}/branch")
async def branch_at(session_id: str, body: dict):
    """Fork the conversation at a specific turn."""
    conv = sessions.get(session_id)
    if not conv:
        return {"error": "Session not found"}, 404
    new_conv = conv.branch_at(body["turn_id"])
    sessions[new_conv.session_id] = new_conv
    return {"new_session_id": new_conv.session_id, "history": new_conv.to_messages()}

@app.post("/chat/{session_id}/regenerate")
async def regenerate_last(session_id: str):
    """Delete the last assistant turn and regenerate."""
    conv = sessions.get(session_id)
    if not conv:
        return {"error": "Session not found"}, 404
    # Remove last assistant turn only (keep user message)
    if conv.turns and conv.turns[-1].role == "assistant":
        conv = Conversation(session_id=session_id, turns=conv.turns[:-1])
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=2048,
        messages=conv.to_messages(),
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text
    import uuid
    conv.turns.append(ConversationTurn(
        role="assistant", content=reply, turn_id=str(uuid.uuid4())
    ))
    sessions[session_id] = conv
    return {"reply": reply}

An inline edit control appears on hover over each user bubble. Clicking it replaces the bubble with a textarea pre-filled with the original text. On submit, the client calls the edit endpoint and replaces all subsequent messages with the new response.
A persistent undo control at the top of the chat removes the last user and assistant turn pair. It is more discoverable than a hover action and works even when the last response is still streaming.

Quick-correction chips give users a one-click way to say 'too long', 'wrong format', or 'not what I meant' without retyping. The server applies the feedback as a correction prompt to Claude without altering the stored history — it appends a correction turn instead.

from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = AsyncAnthropic()

@dataclass
class CorrectionRequest:
    feedback: str        # e.g. "too long", "wrong format", "not what I meant"
    original_response: str
    original_query: str

CORRECTION_SYSTEM = """You are correcting a previous response based on user feedback.
Read the original query, original response, and user feedback.
Produce an improved response that addresses the feedback directly.
Do not explain what you changed — just give the improved response."""

async def apply_correction(req: CorrectionRequest) -> str:
    correction_prompt = (
        f"Original query: {req.original_query}\n\n"
        f"Original response:\n{req.original_response}\n\n"
        f"User feedback: {req.feedback}\n\n"
        f"Please provide an improved response."
    )
    result = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=CORRECTION_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": correction_prompt}],
    )
    return result.content[0].text

Branching creates a new session that shares history up to a branch point. This lets users explore 'what if I had asked differently' without losing the original thread. Branches are displayed in the session sidebar.

# Branch is already implemented in the Conversation.branch_at() method above.
# In the client: when a user clicks "Branch from here" on an assistant message:
async def handle_branch(session_id: str, turn_id: str, initial_message: str):
    # 1. Call /branch to fork the history
    resp = await fetch(f"/chat/{session_id}/branch",
                       method="POST", json={"turn_id": turn_id})
    new_sid = resp["new_session_id"]
    # 2. Navigate to the new session
    # 3. Optionally send the initial_message in the new session
    return new_sid
Tracking Correction Events for Quality Improvement

Log every correction action with what was corrected and why. This data drives prompt improvements — high edit rates on specific topics indicate the agent's default behaviour needs tuning for those cases.

import pytest
from app import Conversation, ConversationTurn
import uuid

def make_conv(*pairs):
    turns = []
    for role, content in pairs:
        turns.append(ConversationTurn(role=role, content=content, turn_id=str(uuid.uuid4())))
    return Conversation(session_id="test", turns=turns)

def test_edit_turn_truncates_history():
    conv = make_conv(("user","hi"), ("assistant","hello"), ("user","bye"), ("assistant","goodbye"))
    turn_id = conv.turns[2].turn_id   # "bye" user turn
    edited = conv.edit_turn(turn_id, "see you")
    assert len(edited.turns) == 3   # up to and including edited turn
    assert edited.turns[2].content == "see you"

def test_undo_removes_last_pair():
    conv = make_conv(("user","hi"), ("assistant","hello"), ("user","bye"), ("assistant","goodbye"))
    undone = conv.undo_last_turn()
    assert len(undone.turns) == 2
    assert undone.turns[-1].content == "hello"

def test_branch_creates_new_session():
    conv = make_conv(("user","A"), ("assistant","B"), ("user","C"), ("assistant","D"))
    branch_id = conv.turns[2].turn_id   # "C"
    branch = conv.branch_at(branch_id)
    assert branch.session_id != conv.session_id
    assert len(branch.turns) == 2   # only "A" and "B"

def test_edit_nonexistent_turn_raises():
    conv = make_conv(("user","hi"), ("assistant","hello"))
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        conv.edit_turn("does-not-exist", "new")

User correction patterns transform a one-shot chat into an iterative collaboration. Edit and undo operate on history directly and force Claude to regenerate from the corrected point. Branching creates a parallel thread for exploration. Regenerate retries the last response in-place. Inline feedback chips apply targeted corrections without rewriting history. Log all correction events — they are the clearest signal available about where the agent's default behaviour diverges from what users actually want.
